seoTitle: How to Add MX Records in Hostinger to Connect Gmail
metaDescription: Learn how to add MX records in Hostinger's DNS zone to route your domain's email through Gmail, Zoho Mail, or another provider without downtime.
intro: If you've registered a domain with Hostinger but want your email handled by Gmail, Zoho Mail, or another provider, you need to configure MX records correctly. This guide walks through adding those records inside Hostinger's DNS Zone Editor so mail sent to your domain actually reaches your inbox. Getting the mail server value, priority, and TTL right the first time saves you from bounced emails and long propagation delays.

steps.heading: Log in to your Hostinger account
steps.body: Go to hostinger.com and sign in to reach your hPanel dashboard, where all domain and hosting management happens.

steps.heading: Open your domain's settings
steps.body: In hPanel, click Domains to see your Domain Portfolio, then click Manage next to the domain you want to configure.

steps.heading: Find the DNS Zone Editor
steps.body: On the Domain Management page, open the DNS / Name Server settings, often labeled DNS Zone Editor, where MX records are added or changed.

steps.heading: Add your MX record
steps.body: Select MX as the record type, then enter the mail server value your provider gives you, a priority number, and a TTL, then save the record.

steps.heading: Wait for propagation and test
steps.body: DNS changes can take a few minutes to a few hours to fully propagate; send a test email or use a DNS lookup tool to confirm the MX record is live.

faq.question: How long does it take for MX records to work after adding them in Hostinger?
faq.answer: Propagation usually takes anywhere from a few minutes up to several hours, depending on your previous DNS settings and TTL value.

faq.question: Can I use Gmail and Zoho Mail on the same Hostinger domain?
faq.answer: You can only route mail through one email provider at a time per domain, since MX records point to a single mail server setup; switching providers means replacing the old MX records.

faq.question: What priority number should I use for MX records?
faq.answer: Lower numbers indicate higher priority; most providers tell you the exact priority to enter, such as 1, 5, or 10, for each mail server they list.

faq.question: Why isn't my email working after adding MX records?
faq.answer: This is usually a propagation delay or a typo in the mail server value or priority, so double-check the values against your provider's instructions and wait before testing again.
